main_text: Introduction Atopy is defined as a tendency to become sensitised and produce IgE antibodies in response to ordinary exposure to allergens, usually proteins (1) . Early life events, including those before birth, influence the development of atopy. Those at the atopic response extreme already have elevated specific IgE at birth (2) . T-helper lymphocytes (Th) play a central role in atopic development (3, 4) . Th lymphocytes differentiate into subgroups in response to the maturity of the cells presenting antigens to them and the cytokines to which they are exposed (5) . Th2 lymphocytes are the Th subset principally involved in the development of atopy (6) . Vitamin D receptors are present on many immune cell types, with vitamin D having effects on cells within both the innate and adaptive components of the immune system. The biologically active form of vitamin D, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (1,25-(OH) 2 7 D), causes macrophages and monocytes to proliferate and modulates their cytokine production ( ). Under the influence of 1,25-(OH) 2 D dendritic cells reduce expression of costimulatory molecules, with inhibition of interleukin 12induced Th1 lymphocyte skewing. In the presence of 1,25-(OH) 2 5 D, dendritic cells become more tolerogenic, and promote preferential expansion of regulatory T lymphocytes ( , 8). Regulatory T lymphocytes decrease production of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interferon gamma, and increase production of immune-modulatory cytokines interleukin 10 and transforming growth factor beta (8) . The relationship between vitamin D 9 status and atopy is non-linear ( ). In comparison with children with a cord blood 25 Prevention of atopy could therefore theoretically be achieved by maintaining normal vitamin D status during the life phase when atopy develops. We recently completed a randomised, doubleblind, placebo-controlled parallel-group trial of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy and infancy in New Zealand (NZ) (11) . In NZ there is a sun-avoidance public health policy, the diet has a low vitamin D content and vitamin D 12 supplementation is used infrequently ( , 13). Hence vitamin D deficiency is prevalent with 57% of newborns having cord blood 25(OH)D 14 concentrations <50 nmol/L ( ). Both asthma and atopy are highly prevalent in NZ with 25% of six-to-seven year olds having reported asthma (15) , and 35% of 11-to-12 year olds being atopic (16) . Our objective was toAuthor Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reserved determine whether vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy and infancy prevents aeroallergen sensitisation and atopic respiratory illness presentations to primary care.Author Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reservedMethodsTrial design We conducted a previously described randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group study in Auckland, NZ (latitude 36°S) (11, 17) . The objective of this study was to determine the vitamin D dose necessary to achieve 25(OH)D concentration ≥ 50 nmol/L during infancy (11) . We randomly assigned pregnant mothers (from enrolment at 27 weeks gestation to birth) and their infants (from birth to age 6 months), to one of three groups: daily placebo; lower-dose; or higherdose oral vitamin D (Figure 1 ). Ethical approval was from the Ministry of Health ethics committee (NTX09/11/101). We obtained written informed consent from all participating women. Registration was with the Australian NZ Clinical Trials Registry (ACTRN12610000483055).Participants We recruited women from an urban primary care maternity clinic from April 2010 to July 2011. We excluded women taking vitamin D supplementation >200 IU/day, and those with any serious pregnancy complication (11, 17) .Interventions Pregnant women took one drop of placebo or 1000 IU or 2000 IU of vitamin D per day (enrolment to birth) and infants took placebo or 400 IU or 800 IU of vitamin D per day (birth to age 6 months). Reported adherence was 95% during pregnancy and 86% during infancy (11) .Outcomes The findings presented here are secondary analyses of sensitisation to airborne allergens and acute primary healthcare visits for respiratory illnesses. Measurement of aeroallergen sensitisation and healthcare visits required additional funding obtained when the children were, on average, 18 months old.Sample size Sample size calculations were based upon the primary study protocol objective of achieving a serum 25(OH)D concentration ≥50 nmol/L (11) .Randomisation and blinding Randomisation was performed by the study biostatistician (AS) (11) . Study participants and research staff remained unaware of treatment allocations throughout the recruitment, enrolment, treatment and data acquisition phases.Author Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reservedData collection We interviewed enrolled women during pregnancy and when their infant was 2, 4, 6, and 18 months old. For this study, we collected data describing demographics, adherence, mother's food consumption, infant feeding, sunlight exposure, primary care visits for asthma, and drugs prescribed (11, 17) . The food frequency questionnaire (FFQ) used contained 12 food items that captured the major dietary sources of vitamin D 18 in the NZ food supply ( ). At the assessment completed when the children were 18 months old, we obtained a capillary blood sample from each child and measured serum 25(OH)D concentration (11, 17) . We determined aeroallergen sensitisation by skin prick testing (SPT) to house dust mite, cat and grass pollens (Supplementary Table 1 ) (19) ; and semi-quantitative measurement (ImmunoCAP, Pharmacia, Uppsala, Sweden) of specific serum IgE antibodies to antigens in the following groups: animal; polcalcin; grass, tree, and weed pollens; mould; mites; and cockroach (Supplementary Table 1 ) (20) . We defined a positive SPT response as a wheal diameter ≥3mm larger than the negative control (21) . We categorised IgE responses as undetectable (ISAC Standardized Units (ISU) 0-0•3), low (ISU >0•3-1•0), moderate/high (ISU >1-15) and very high (ISU >15). We reviewed the primary care records for the doctor diagnosis assigned at each acute respiratory illness visit. In NZ each patient's primary care file includes a free text record completed by the doctor at each visit and stored in an electronic practice management system. Each patient's records are identified by their National Health Index number, a unique identifier assigned to each person in NZ at their first contact with the healthcare system. We read each child's primary care record and assigned each respiratory illness visit to one of nine diagnostic categories based upon the doctor's written record. These categories were a cold, otitis media, an upper respiratory infection, croup, asthma, bronchitis, bronchiolitis, a wheezy lower respiratory infection or fever and cough. We determined whether salbutamol or prednisone were prescribed at each visit.Statistical methods Analyses were performed on an intention to treat basis using SAS version 9.4 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA) and R version 3.1.3 (R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria). We described maternal and child characteristics, including those specifically relevant to vitamin D status. We compared maternal and child characteristics between study groups and also between groups based upon whether they had serum obtained at age 18 months. We used the chi-square and Fisher's exact tests to compare proportions, and the Wilcoxon rank sum test to compare continuous data. For the comparison of the semi-quantitative specific IgE responses, we combined the moderate/high and very high categories. For antigens to which measureable serum IgE antibody Author Manuscript responses were present in more than 5% of the children, the proportion in each study group with IgE antibodies present were compared using relative risks (RR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI). The number needed to treat (NNT) to prevent sensitisation was calculated as 1/(absolute risk reduction).Author Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reservedResultsCharacteristics of study participants (Table 1) Enrolment of the pregnant women was evenly distributed by season. Fifty-nine (23%) of the mothers and 28 (11%) of the fathers had a history of doctor-diagnosed asthma. Median maternal daily dietary vitamin D As previously described, at enrolment mean serum 25(OH)D intake during the month prior to study enrolment was 2•9 μg/day. While virtually all (95%) enrolled children were breastfed at birth, 47% had commenced infant milk formula prior to age six months. (63 nmol/L) did not differ between study groups (P=0 •19) . At 36 weeks gestation and in cord blood samples mean serum 25(OH)D was higher in both the lower-and higher-dose vitamin D groups than in the placebo group. In the infants at two, four and six months of age mean serum 25(OH)D differed between the three study groups with the higher-dose vitamin D group having the highest and the placebo group the lowest 25(OH)D 11 concentrations ( ). At age 18 months, 12 months after completion of the intervention, mean serum25(OH)D Comparisons between those in whom atopy was versus was not measured at age 18 months (61 nmol/L) did not differ between study groups (P=0•30). At age 18 months serum was obtained from 186 (75%) children of the 260 women enrolled (Figure 1 ). In comparison with those in whom specific IgE concentration was measured, the mothers of children in whom it was not were older (28 vs. 25 years, P<0•001) but did not otherwise differ with respect to season of enrolment, demographics, health, vitamin D intake or sunlight exposure. Nor were there differences in cord blood 25(OH)D Skin prick test results and serum IgE concentration comparisons between placebo, lower-and higher-dose vitamin D supplementation groups concentration or infant feeding (Supplementary Table 2 ). Skin prick test (SPT) results for the three aeroallergens (HDM, cat, grass pollen) were available for 184 of the 186 children from whom serum was obtained at age 18 months. The proportion with SPT positivity varied between study groups for HDM (P=0•03) but not for cat (P=0•74) or grass pollen (P=0•99). In comparison with children randomised to the placebo group, a smaller portion of those randomised to the higher-dose (0% vs. 9%, P=0•03), but not the lower-dose (3% vs. 9%, P=0 •28) vitamin D group were SPT positive to HDM (Table 2 ). With the exception of mites (26/186, 14%) fewer than 5% of children had a measureable serum specific IgE response to each antigen group: animals (6/186, 3%); grass pollen (0/186, 0%); treeAuthor Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reserved pollen (0/186, 0%); weed pollen (1/186, 1%); mould (2/186, 1%); and cockroach (0/186, 0%) Therefore we only proceeded with between group comparisons for the mite antigen group. Measureable IgE antibodies to any mite antigen were present in 13 (20%) of the children in the placebo group, 9 (14%) of the lower-dose and 4 (7%) of the higher-dose vitamin D groups. In comparison with the placebo group, the risk of mite antigen sensitisation was reduced in the higherdose (RR=0.34, 95% CI 0.12-0.94) but not the lower-dose (RR=0.71, 95% CI 0.33-0.1.52) vitamin D groups. The NNT for the higher-dose vitamin D group was 8 (95% CI 4 to 109) and for the lower-dose vitamin D group was 18 (95% CI -13 to 6).The proportion of children with measureable Comparison of primary care visits and of salbutamol and prednisone prescribing between placebo, lower-and higher-dose vitamin D supplementation groups (Table 4 ) The primary care visit audit occurred at a median age of 18 (IQR, 14-21) months (placebo, 18; lowerdose vitamin D, 18; higher-dose vitamin D, 17 months; P=0.59). The proportion of children with a primary care visit where asthma was recorded as being present, but not where any of the other acute respiratory illness diagnoses were recorded, varied by study group (P=0•002), as did the number of primary care visits per child where asthma was recorded as being present (P=0•004). The proportion of children who, at any acute primary care respiratory visit, were prescribed salbutamol did not differ by study group: placebo 32%, lower-dose vitamin D 30%, higher-dose vitamin D 28%, P=0•81), but the proportion who were prescribed prednisone did: placebo 27%, lower-dose vitamin D 14%, higher-dose vitamin D 15%, P=0•04). The proportion of children with a primary care visit recorded as being due to asthma at which salbutamol (P=0•02) or prednisone (P=0•02) was prescribed varied by study group. The number of primary care visits per child whereAuthor Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reserved asthma was recorded and salbutamol (P=0•004) or prednisone (P=0•03) was prescribed also varied by study group. Of the 26 (14%) children with serum specific IgE responses to a mite antigen, 13 (50%) were in the placebo group, nine (35%) in the lower-dose and four (15%) in the higher-dose vitamin D group. Of these, one child in the placebo group, but none in either of the vitamin D supplementation groups, made any primary care visits described by the doctor as being for asthma. No association was evident between the presence of HDM sensitisation and having a primary care visit described by the doctor as being for asthma (P=0.79).Author Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reservedDiscussionStatement of principal findings In this post-hoc analysis of a randomised, placebo-controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation during the third trimester of pregnancy and the first six months of infancy we showed that vitamin D supplementation resulted in a decrease in the proportion of children sensitised to HDM at age 18 months. The number of mite antigens to which the children were sensitised and the magnitude of IgE responses to these mite antigens were both lower in the vitamin D supplemented groups. In comparison with the placebo group the proportion of children with any primary care visits for which asthma was recorded as being present and the number of such primary care asthma visits per child were smaller in the vitamin D supplemented groups; as was the proportion of these primary care asthma visits and the number of visits per child for which salbutamol or prednisone were prescribed.Strengths and weaknesses of the study Vitamin D was given from 27 weeks gestation and continued until the infants were six months old. Atopic sensitisation can start in utero (2) . Children of atopic parents who have evidence at birth of sensitisation to mites are at increased risk of developing allergic symptoms by age one year (22) . Interventions that seek to prevent atopy sensitisation are therefore likely to need to begin before birth. As immune responses to allergens can be detected as early as 22 weeks gestation, vitamin D supplementation may have been more effective if started earlier in pregnancy (23) . We used a daily dose of vitamin D and thus avoided the wide variations in serum 25(OH)D concentration seen when large intermittent vitamin D doses are used and which are believed to cause immune responses that are not beneficial to the host (24, 25) . We maintained study blinding until the children were 18 months old and at this age measured atopic sensitisation and acute primary care visits. The electronic primary care records we audited to describe primary care asthma and other respiratory illness visits were not coded. Hence they were not subject to the under-estimations, by approximately 25%, of primary care asthma visits in preschool aged children that result when only coded primary care data are used (26) . As the description of primary care visits was not one of the primary objectives of the trial, we would expect the care received by the children in this study to reflect usual practice. There is considerable variability between doctors in their assignment of specific diagnoses to episodes of acute respiratory illness in young children (27) . To an extent such variability is siteAuthor Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reserved specific with, for example, presentation to some emergency departments being associated with an increased likelihood of a diagnosis of asthma being made in young children presenting with acute lower respiratory tract illness symptoms (27) . That the children enrolled were seen at 126 different primary practices meant that any such institutional bias will have been addressed adequately by randomisation. Strengths and weaknesses in relation to other studies, discussing particularly any differences in results Although no studies to date have reported the effect of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy and early infancy on asthma during childhood, placebo controlled clinical trials have shown efficacy of vitamin D supplementation in children with asthma. A meta-analysis, published in 2015 (28) , that included data from five clinical trials which used a vitamin D doses ranging from 500 to 2000 IU/day in children aged 5 to 18 years, showed a statistically significant reduction in asthma exacerbations (29) (30) (31) (32) (33) . One further trial published since this meta-analysis, which enrolled 130 individuals aged 10 to 50 years and randomly assigned them vitamin D or placebo, showed a significant improvement in lung function after 24 weeks of the intervention (34) . This was a post-hoc analysis performed on additional data, measurement of SPT and serum IgE responses to aeroallergens at age 18 months. We collected data specifically for this analysis and prior to study unblinding. Thus, we did not initiate these analyses following an inspection of already collected data, which is the principle methodological weakness of a post-hoc approach (35) .Meaning of the study: possible mechanisms and implications for clinicians or policymakers Based upon specific serum IgE measurement the proportion of children sensitised to mites (14%) was greater than for all other aeroallergens tests (all <5%). In children, reported rates of mite antigen sensitisation range from 5% to 28% with population based estimates from NZ being at the higher end of this range (36) (37) (38) . Internationally, mite antigen sensitisation is one of the most prevalent forms of aeroallergen sensitisation (38) . The differences between study groups were seen to the antigens of the house dust mites D. farinae and D. pteronyssinus, but not to those of the storage mites Blomia and Lepidoglyphus which, while major sensitising aeroallergen in some countries, are not in NZ (39, 40) . Sensitisation to mites is associated with an increased risk of childhood asthma as shown in cohort studies where asthma prevalence in children was measured at an older age than in this study, for example seven and 11 years (41, 42) . That no association was evident is likely a reflection of the younger age at which we estimated asthma prevalence and the difficulty in diagnosing asthma in the first two years of life.Author Manuscript This article is protected by copyright. All rights reserved Our demonstration that vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy and infancy prevents HDM sensitisation is consistent with vitamin D having immune modulatory functions. Vitamin D promotes the induction of regulatory T-cells which in turn can prevent the pathological immune responses to antigens that lead to clinical allergic disease (8) . In animal models, exposure to adequate vitamin D prior to sensitisation prevents cellular airway responses to aeroallergens (43) .Unanswered questions and future research As rates of aeroallergen antigen sensitisation increase with increasing age, between-group differences may emerge for sensitisation to the other aeroallergens tested and in clinical manifestations of allergic disease (44) . Longer-term follow-up is also important to ensure that vitamin D supplementation does not place some individuals at increased risk of atopic sensitisation and allergic disease. In birth cohort studies from the United States (10) and Germany (45) , both high (≥100 nmol/L) and low (<50 nmol/L) cord blood 25(OH)D concentrations were associated with an increased risk of aeroallergen sensitisation at age five years (10) , and higher maternal 25(OH)D concentration during late pregnancy was associated with an increased risk of food allergen sensitisation at age two years (45) . Children who become atopic are at increased risk of asthma and of having more severe and persistent asthma (46, 47) . We do not know if the differences in mite sensitisation and primary care asthma visits that we observed to age 18 months, as a result of vitamin D supplementation during late pregnancy and early infancy, are a temporary phenomenon or one that has lifelong consequences in terms of an individual's risk of developing asthma. The diagnosis of asthma is very insecure at age 18 months. Follow-up to an age when the diagnosis of asthma can be made with greater precision is necessary to determine the true effect of pregnancy and early infancy interventions on asthma prevalence (48) . Our findings do indicate though the potential for daily supplemental vitamin D to prevent asthma in populations where both vitamin D deficiency and asthma are prevalent. Studies with more robust asthma case definitions need to follow these preliminary findings. Cameron Grant conceived and designed the study, developed the data collection instruments, analysed and interpreted the data and completed the first and final drafts of the manuscript.Statement of contribution by each named author Julian Crane developed the data collection instruments, reviewed and revised the manuscript and approved the final manuscript as submitted. In comparison with the placebo group, a smaller proportion of children in the lower-dose vitamin D group had measurable IgE antibodies to Der-f2 (P=0•03) and Der-p2 (P=0•03); and a smaller proportion of children in the higher-dose vitamin D group had measurable IgE antibodies to Der-f1 (P=0•003), Der-f2 (P=0•02) and Der-p1 (P=0•009). Semi-quantitative IgE responses varied between study groups to the mite antigens Der-f1 (P=0•01), Der-f2 (P=0•004), Der-p1 (P=0•02) and Der-p2 (P=0•001) (Figure 2). In comparison with the placebo group, the semi-quantitative IgE responses were lower in the lower-dose vitamin D group to the Der-f2 (P=0•03) and Der-p2 (P=0•03) antigens; and in the higher-dose vitamin D group to the Der-f1 (P=0•002), Der-f2 (P=0•009), Der-p1 (P=0•01) and Der-p2 (P=0•004) antigens.Figure 1 . 1 Figure 1. title: Vitamin D activity of breast milk in women randomly assigned to vitamin D3 supplementation during pregnancy
abstract: Background: Human milk is typically low in vitamin D activity (VDA). Whether the vitamin D content of breast milk at birth can be increased by supplementing the mother during pregnancy has not been reported to the best of our knowledge. Objective: We examined the effect of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy on breast-milk VDA in the first 2 mo of lactation. Design: Breast-milk samples were obtained from women who were enrolled in a randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy. Pregnant women were enrolled at 27 wk of gestation and randomly assigned to the following 3 groups: a placebo group, a group who received one dosage of daily oral vitamin D 3 (1000 IU), or a group who received 2 dosages of daily oral vitamin D 3 (2000 IU). Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D] was measured at enrollment, at 36 wk of gestation, and in cord blood at birth. Study participants who were breastfeeding were invited to provide breast-milk samples for VDA measurement [concentration of vitamin D 2 , vitamin D 3 , 25(OH)D 2 , and 25(OH)D 3 ] at 2 wk and 2 mo postpartum. A linear mixed model was used to compare breast-milk VDA between the 3 study groups. Results: A total of 75 women provided breast-milk samples (44 women provided breast-milk samples at both 2 wk and 2 mo postpartum). The mean (95% CI) VDA at age 2 wk was 52 IU/L (12, 217 IU/L) in the placebo group, 51 IU/L (17, 151 IU/L) in the 1000-IU group, and 74 IU/L (25, 221 IU/L) in the 2000-IU group; and at age 2 mo, the mean (95% CI) VDA was 45 IU/L (16, 124 IU/L), 43 IU/L (18, 103 IU/L), and 58 IU/L (15, 224 IU/L), respectively. There was no significant interaction in VDA between the sample-collection time and treatment (P = 0.61), but there was a difference between lower-and higher-dosage treatment groups (P = 0.04). Conclusion: Maternal vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy of 2000 IU/d (compared with 1000 IU/d and with a placebo) results in a higher VDA of breast milk $2 mo postpartum. This trial was registered at the Australian New Zealand Clinical Trials Registry as ACTRN12610000483055.
text: Background: Human milk is typically low in vitamin D activity (VDA). Whether the vitamin D content of breast milk at birth can be increased by supplementing the mother during pregnancy has not been reported to the best of our knowledge. Objective: We examined the effect of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy on breast-milk VDA in the first 2 mo of lactation. Design: Breast-milk samples were obtained from women who were enrolled in a randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy. Pregnant women were enrolled at 27 wk of gestation and randomly assigned to the following 3 groups: a placebo group, a group who received one dosage of daily oral vitamin D 3 (1000 IU), or a group who received 2 dosages of daily oral vitamin D 3 (2000 IU). Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D] was measured at enrollment, at 36 wk of gestation, and in cord blood at birth. Study participants who were breastfeeding were invited to provide breast-milk samples for VDA measurement [concentration of vitamin D 2 , vitamin D 3 , 25(OH)D 2 , and 25(OH)D 3 ] at 2 wk and 2 mo postpartum. A linear mixed model was used to compare breast-milk VDA between the 3 study groups. Results: A total of 75 women provided breast-milk samples (44 women provided breast-milk samples at both 2 wk and 2 mo postpartum). The mean (95% CI) VDA at age 2 wk was 52 IU/L (12, 217 IU/L) in the placebo group, 51 IU/L (17, 151 IU/L) in the 1000-IU group, and 74 IU/L (25, 221 IU/L) in the 2000-IU group; and at age 2 mo, the mean (95% CI) VDA was 45 IU/L (16, 124 IU/L), 43 IU/L (18, 103 IU/L), and 58 IU/L (15, 224 IU/L), respectively. There was no significant interaction in VDA between the sample-collection time and treatment (P = 0.61), but there was a difference between lower-and higher-dosage treatment groups (P = 0.04). This trial was registered at the Australian New Zealand Clinical Trials Registry as ACTRN12610000483055.
main_text: INTRODUCTION Breastfed infants of vitamin D-sufficient mothers are protected from vitamin D-deficiency rickets during the first few months of life because vitamin D metabolites, especially 25hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D], 11 cross the placenta, resulting in neonatal 25(OH)D concentrations that are approximately twothirds that of maternal 25(OH)D concentrations during pregnancy (1, 2) . Because the half-life of serum 25(OH)D is approximately 3 wk, neonates who do not receive an exogenous supply of vitamin D during the first weeks of life will experience decreases in their serum 25(OH)D concentrations from w2 mo of age (3) . Potential sources of vitamin D include UVB exposure, vitamin D supplements, infant milk formula, and breast milk (3) (4) (5) . Human milk from a vitamin D-sufficient mother provides ,100 IU total vitamin D activity (VDA) [i.e., concentration of vitamin D 2 , vitamin D 3 , 25(OH)D 2 , and 25(OH)D 3 ]/d (6) (7) (8) (9) . The recommendation to exclusively breastfeed infants to 6 mo of age has led several countries to introduce vitamin D supplementation of breastfed infants (10) . This supplementation allows exclusively breastfed infants to achieve vitamin D intakes $400 IU/d (10) . The possibility of supplementing the lactating mother has been considered as an alternative to supplementing the infant. Highdose vitamin D supplementation of the mother during lactation results in an increase in the total VDA of breast milk (11, 12) with 4000 IU/d being more effective than 2000 IU/d for increasing both maternal and infant serum 25(OH) concentrations and breast-milk VDA (11) . Another potential method for increasing the vitamin D concentration of breast milk at birth is by supplementing the mother during pregnancy and, thereby, providing adequate vitamin D to the breastfeeding neonate. Our aim was to examine the effect of vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy on breast-milk VDA in the first 2 mo of lactation. To our knowledge, there are no published data on this topic.METHODSStudy design, setting, and sample This study was performed within a randomized, doubleblinded, placebo-controlled trial of vitamin D 3 supplementation in pregnancy and infancy (13) in Auckland, New Zealand. Ethical approval was obtained from the Regional Ministry of Health Ethics Committee (Northern X committee; application NTX09/11/101). Written informed consent was obtained from all participating women. This trial was registered at the Australian New Zealand Clinical Trials Registry at ACTRN12610000483055. Two-hundred sixty pregnant women and their infants were randomly assigned to one of 3 groups as follows: a placebo group, a group who received one dosage of daily oral vitamin D 3 (1000 IU), or a group who received 2 dosages of daily oral vitamin D 3 (2000 IU) (Figure 1 ). More than 90% of the women reported adherence with the daily study medication protocol, and adherence did not differ between study groups (13) . We invited women who were enrolled in this trial and whose intention was to exclusively breastfeed to provide a breast-milk sample 2 wk and 2 mo postpartum (Figure 1 ).Data collection Face-to-face interviews were completed with women at enrollment at 27 wk of gestation, at 36 wk of gestation, and when their infants were 2 mo old. Data were collected on maternal age, ethnicity, education, weight and height, gestation, and adherence to the study medication. Maternal vitamin D-supplement use and the time spent outdoors were recorded. In New Zealand adults, reported sunlight exposure is correlated with the serum 25(OH) D concentration (14) . Maternal dietary vitamin D intake was determined from a food-frequency questionnaire (FFQ). The FFQ contained 12 food items that captured the major dietary sources of vitamin D in the New Zealand food supply, which were derived from the New Zealand Food Composition Database (15). The frequency of consumption over the previous 4 wk of each food item was recorded (with the use of standard foodserving sizes). Frequency responses were recorded as never, less than monthly, 1-3 times/mo, weekly, 2-4 times/wk, 5-6 times/ wk, daily, and $2 times/d. The frequency of consumption of each food item in the FFQ was converted to intake of vitamin D expressed as mg/d by multiplying the vitamin D concentration of the standard serving size of each food as specified in the FFQ by the following values for each frequency option: never or ,1 time/mo = 0; 1-3 times/mo = 0.1; 1 time/wk = 0.14; 2-4 times/ wk = 0.4; 5-6 times/ week = 0.8; 1 time/d = 1.0; and $2 times/ d = 2. Maternal serum 25(OH)D was measured at 27 and 36 wk of gestation with the use of isotope-dilution liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) in a vitamin D External Quality Assurance Scheme-certified laboratory (16) .Breast-milk sample collection Participants used a standard protocol for the collection of breast-milk samples for the subsequent analysis of vitamin D concentrations (17) . Samples were collected either manually or with the use of a human-milk pump. Breast milk was collected directly into a small sampling bottle that was provided to the mothers. If a pump was used, milk was collected into a clean container that came with the pump and was poured into the sampling bottle. Breasts and hands were cleaned, but soap and lotions were avoided because they may have contained chemicals that could interfere with the measurement of breast-milk VDA. The sample was collected at the regular feeding time in the morning, 2 h after the previous breastfeed (this method was used to allow for some consistency between samples). A full breast was expressed to ensure that the hindmilk was collected. The samples were stored in the participant's freezer until collection. Samples were transferred to a 2808C freezer before being shipped on dry ice to the laboratory for analyses.Breast-milk analyses and measurement of VDA Breast-milk VDA was assessed by summing the concentration of vitamin D 2 , vitamin D 3 , 25(OH)D 2 , and 25(OH)D 3 in breast milk and using the following conversion: 1 IU vitamin D = 25 ng. In addition, 1 IU 25(OH)D was equal to 5 ng and 1.030 g/mL for the specific gravity of milk (18) . Breast-milk concentrations of vitamin D 2 , vitamin D 3 , 25(OH)D 2 , and 25(OH)D 3 were determined with the use of LC-MS/MS techniques as described previously (Heartland Assays Inc.) (19) . Methanolic potassium hydroxide was added to each milk sample (5-10 mL) followed by spiking with [6, 19, 19-d 3 ]-vitamin D 3 and [6, 19, 19-d 3 ]-25(OH)D 3 . Samples were mixed on a vortex and saponified. After 0.5-2 h at 608C, the lipids were extracted with methylene chloride. The lipid extracts were dried under a vacuum and applied to a 0.5-g silica solid-phase extraction column. Vitamin D and 25(OH)D were eluted and collected separately from the solid-phase extraction column. Solvent was removed under a vacuum, and samples were purified with the use of 2 chemically different HPLC systems. The final quantitation of the vitamin D and 25(OH)D was achieved by LC-MS/MS with the use of an Agilent 1200 Series Rapid Resolution LC system (Agilent Technologies) coupled with an Agilent 6460 Triple Quadrupole LC/MS with multimode ionization (Agilent Technologies). Calibration curves were constructed for vitamin D and 25(OH)D. Excellent linearity was achieved for vitamin D and 25(OH)D with R 2 values that were very close to 1.00. The day-to-day (interassay and intra-assay) CVs for quantification were #12%. The limit of detection for each assay was 10 pg/mL.Statistical analyses With the use of the data from Hollis and Wagner (11) , we estimated that the SD of breast-milk VDA would be w12 IU/L. With 25 subjects in each treatment group, a difference of 12 IU/L between 2 supplementation amounts could be detected with 80% power at the 5% level of significance. Because distributions of VDA values were skewed, we used a logarithmic transformation to stabilize variances and lessen the effect of extreme values. Estimates were back transformed for reporting. All statistical analyses were performed with the use of SAS version 9.3 software (SAS Institute). To compare the VDA in breast milk collected at birth and 2 mo between the 3 treatment groups, we used a linear mixed model with the participant as the random effect and used an autoregressive correlation structure for the 2 time periods. Two orthogonal contrasts of the supplementation amounts allowed for the assessment of the placebo treatment compared with the active treatment and 1000 IU compared with 2000 IU. The model also included the following 3 potential confounding factors: ethnicity (in 4 categories), the time of year that the samples were taken, and the self-reported maternal sunlight exposure. The association between maternal serum 25(OH)D concentrations at 36 wk of gestation and breast-milk VDA was described with the use of Spearman correlation coefficients.RESULTS Breast-milk samples were collected from a total of 75 women (Figure 1 ), 44 of whom provided breast-milk samples at both 2 wk and 2 mo postpartum. The 31 women who provided one sample only were from the following 2 groups: 27 women who provided a sample at 2 wk (but not 2 mo) because they did not continue breastfeeding until 2 mo, and 4 women who provided a sample at 2 mo (but not 2 wk) because they had some difficulties establishing breast feeding (Figure 1 ). Table 1 shows the baseline characteristics of the 75 mothers who provided a breast-milk sample compared with the 118 mothers from whom a breast-milk sample was not obtained. In mothers who provided a breast-milk sample compared with in mothers who did not provide a breast-milk sample, the median maternal serum 25(OH)D concentration at 36 wk of gestation (97 compared with 77 nmol/L, respectively; P , 0.001) and median cord blood 25(OH)D concentration (64 compared with 46 nmol/L, respectively; P , 0.001) were higher . Table 2 shows the characteristics of the 75 study participants from whom breast-milk samples were obtained. The median maternal age at enrollment was 30 y, and gestation was 27 wk. Median serum 25(OH)D concentrations at 36 wk of gestation were higher in the supplemented groups than in the placebo group (110 compared with 71 nmol/L, respectively; P = 0.003) but did not differ between higher-and lower-dose-vitamin D groups (122 compared with 106 nmol/L, respectively; P = 0.24). Median serum 25(OH)D concentrations in cord blood samples differed between supplemented and placebo groups (66 compared with 44 nmol/L, respectively; P = 0.002) and between higher-and lower-dose-vitamin D groups (78 compared with 64 nmol/L, respectively; P = 0.03). Breast-milk VDA was comprised of vitamin D 3 and 25(OH) D 3 ; the concentrations of vitamin D 2 and 25(OH)D 2 were undetectable (Supplemental Table 1 2 illustrates the differences in breast-milk VDA at each time point and between supplemented groups. There was no significant interaction between the samplecollection time and the study group on VDA (P = 0.61). The 2 orthogonal contrasts between the treatment groups showed that the VDA in the placebo group was not different from that in the vitamin D-supplemented groups (16% difference; 95% CI: 210%, 50% difference; P = 0.24), but the VDA in the higher-dose-vitamin D group was greater than that in the lower-dose-vitamin D group (39% difference; 95% CI: 4%, 87% difference; P = 0.04) To assess the effect of extreme values on the results, the analyses were repeated with the use of a series of VDA cutoffs to exclude extreme values. The only substantial effect that the removal of extreme values had was to expose a possible difference in the VDA between the placebo group and the groups who received vitamin D supplementation. When the 3 breast-milk samples with the highest VDA were removed from the analysis, the percentage difference between the vitamin D groups and the placebo group was 45% (95% CI: 14%, 83%; P = 0.002). With these 3 samples removed from the analysis, the percentage difference between the 1000-IU vitamin D and 2000-IU vitamin D groups became 30% (95% CI: 1%, 71%; P = 0.07). Of the 71 subjects who provided a breast-milk sample 2 wk postpartum, the 36-wk maternal serum 25(OH)D concentration was correlated with breast-milk VDA (r = 0.40, P , 0.001) (Figure 3 ). Of the 48 subjects who provided a breast-milk sample 2 mo postpartum, the correlation between the 36-wk maternal serum 25(OH)D concentration and breast-milk VDA at 2 mo postpartum was NS (r = 0.28, P = 0.06).DISCUSSION Analyses of breast-milk samples from women who were participating in a randomized trial suggested that the higher amount of vitamin D 3 supplementation (2000 IU/d) compared with the lower amount of vitamin D 3 supplementation (1000 IU/d) resulted in a higher amount of breast-milk VDA, but breast-milk VDA in the supplemented groups was not much greater than what was present in the women who had been randomly assigned to the placebo group. However, when the breast-milk samples with the 3 largest VDA values (.190 IU/L) were excluded from the analysis (2 samples from the placebo group and one sample from the 2000-IU/d group), the difference in VDA between the 2 vitamin D-supplemented groups was slightly reduced, whereas the placebo group had VDA values that were substantially below those that were present in the supplemented groups. Until more is known about the range of VDA values that are valid, we cannot be certain as to which of these alternatives is more robust. The power of our study to detect differences in the VDA between study groups was limited by its small sample size. Our recruitment of subjects into this component of the study was limited by resource constraints and, therefore, did not occur consistently throughout the study. The study was not protected by the randomization of the trial design, and there are some differences in the season of recruitment between subjects who provided a breast-milk sample and those who did not provide a breast-milk sample; the proportion of subjects enrolled during the higher-vitamin D-status seasons (summer and fall) compared with the proportion of subjects enrolled during the lower-vitamin D-status seasons (winter and spring) in New Zealand did not differ (52% compared with 49%, respectively; P = 0.68). Adjustments were made in the analyses for the time of year that the samples were taken and the selfreported maternal sunlight exposure. The higher-supplemented group (2000 IU/d) had one more week of gestation than the lower supplemented group (1000 IU/d) had (40 compared with 39 wk, respectively), and this additional week could have potentially contributed to breast-milk VDA being higher in the higher-dose-vitamin D group than in the lower-dose-vitamin D group. Our study design did not allow us to determine the effect of breast-milk VDA on infant serum 25(OH)D concentrations because the infants of mothers who were given vitamin D supplements also were given vitamin D supplements (13) . We enrolled participants from a country where there is very little vitamin D fortification of food, and we achieved high adherence to the assigned study drops. These factors allowed us to show, for the first time to our knowledge, that vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy has potential as a strategy to prevent vitamin deficiency in young breastfed infants, who are a group that have been recognized to be at increased risk of clinical vitamin D deficiency (2).  1 Proportions were compared with the use of the chi-square or Fisher's exact test, and continuous data were compared with the use of an ANOVA or the Wilcoxon's rank-sum test.   Ethnic groups were those used for the national census. M aori is New Zealand's indigenous population. Ethnicity was defined by the participants. An additional strength of our study was the protocol we used for breast-milk sample collection. We were able to collect a full breast-milk sample from each mother, which included including foremilk and hindmilk, and from 44 mothers, we collected breastmilk samples at both 2 wk and 2 mo. Other studies that have described breast-milk VDA either did not report the methods of breast-milk collection or did not report the collection of both foremilk and hindmilk, which is important because most of VDA is in the fat of the hindmilk (11, 19, 20) . Vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy addresses the pregnancy-associated increased maternal requirement for vitamin D and also, as our larger trial showed, prevents the infant from being born in a vitamin D-deficient state (13) . Although this is the first trial, to our knowledge, to investigate the relation of antenatal maternal vitamin D supplementation with breastmilk VDA, there have been 4 published trials of high-dose maternal vitamin D supplementation during lactation in which breast-milk VDA had been measured (11, 12, 21, 22) . These studies were conducted in Finland, the United Arab Emirates, and the United States. In the study by Ala-Houhala et al. (21) in Finland, women supplemented during winter and spring with 1000 or 2000 IU vitamin D 3 /d had higher breast-milk VDA than nonsupplemented mothers did. In this study, the median breastmilk VDA in hindmilk was greater in mothers who, during winter, were supplemented daily with 2000 compared with 1000 IU (VDA: 92 compared with 51 IU/L, respectively). Breast-milk VDA in mothers who took the 2000-IU supplementation in winter remained lower than that in unsupplemented mothers during the summer. The difference in latitude (Tampere: 618N; Auckland: 378S) is one potential explanation for the seasonal effect on breast-milk VDA not being observed in our study. The studies performed in the Unites States showed that the VDA of breast milk can be increased with a maternal vitamin D supplemental dosage .2000 IU/d (11, 12) . In a study in which 64 lactating women were randomly assigned to receive 2000 or 4000 IU/d of a combination of vitamin D 2 and D 3 , the mean breast-milk VDA, which was measured in the 18 women who completed the study to 3 mo postpartum, increased significantly in both groups from 35 to 70 IU/L in the 2000-IU group and from 40 to 135 IU/L in the 4000-IU group. A subsequent study enrolled 19 exclusively breastfeeding women #1 mo postpartum and randomly assigned them to receive 400 or 6400 IU vitamin D 3 /d for 6 mo. Breast-milk VDA of mothers in the 6400-IU/d group increased (82 IU/L at study month 1 and 873 IU/L at study month 7) but varied little in the 400-IU/d group (60 IU/L at study month 1 and 76 IU/L at study month 7). Infant serum 25(OH)D concentrations at age 7 mo were significantly higher (. 100 nmol/L) in both groups than at baseline and did not vary between groups despite that maternal serum 25(OH)D concentrations were significantly higher in mothers assigned to the 6400-IU/d group. In the study conducted in the United Arab Emirates, as a component of a larger trial, breast-milk samples were collected from women assigned at their first postnatal visit to receive 2000 IU vitamin D 2 /d (22) . VDA was undetectable in the 12 samples collected at baseline, and the mean VDA in the 8 samples collected 3 mo after study enrollment was 51 IU/L. A review published in 2012 of the available studies on maternal vitamin D supplementation concluded that, on the basis of the evidence currently available, it is unlikely that maternal vitamin D supplementation during breastfeeding could increase the VDA of breast milk sufficiently to meet infant requirements (8) . A review published in 2013 stated that doses #10 times the current recommended daily intake of vitamin D in lactation are necessary to produce a sufficient transfer of vitamin D to the infant via breast milk (6) . Previous studies have reported that 25 (OH)D is the major circulating form of vitamin D and is poorly transferred into human milk, whereas the parent vitamin D is readily transferred (4, 23) . However, our study shows that There was no significant interaction between the sample-collection time and study group on VDA (P = 0.631), but there was a difference between study groups (P = 0.04). A linear mixed model was used for the statistical analysis, with the participant as the random effect and with the use of an autoregressive correlation structure for the 2 time periods. The 2 orthogonal contrasts between treatment groups showed that the VDA in the placebo group was not different from that in the vitamin D-supplemented groups (16% difference; 95% CI: 210%, 50% difference; P = 0.24), but the VDA in the higher-dose-vitamin D group was greater than that in the lower-dosevitamin D group (39% difference; 95% CI: 4%, 87% difference; P = 0.04). VDA, vitamin D activity. maternal serum 25(OH)D at 36 wk is correlated with the VDA in breast milk at 2 wk and, possibly, for #2 mo postpartum. Thus, vitamin D supplementation of mothers during pregnancy, which achieves maternal serum 25(OH)D concentrations w100 nmol/L at 36 wk of gestation, improves the vitamin D status of the mother, which, in turn, significantly affects the VDA of breast milk (13) In conclusion, the findings from our study indicate that antenatal vitamin D supplementation is a potential option for preventing vitamin D deficiency during early infancy. However, these findings need to be replicated in other studies. Our data suggest an effect that may be achieved with a vitamin D-supplement dosage (i.e., 2000 IU vitamin D 3 /d) that is substantially smaller than the dosages used in the clinical trial studies to date (12) and within the upper limits of safety as recognized by the Institute of Medicine (10) . Although vitamin D supplementation in the current trial of 2000 IU/d did not produce a breast-milk VDA that met the Nutrient Reference Value (Adequate Intake) for infants of 200 IU/d, it provides evidence that vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy increases breast-milk VDA and may be a potentially important public health strategy for supporting maternal postpartum and early infant health. The authors' responsibilities were as follows-CRW: designed the breast-milk study, developed the data-collection protocol, and wrote the first and final drafts of the manuscript; AWS: designed the randomized controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation, wrote the statistical analyses plan, analyzed and interpreted the data, and reviewed and revised the manuscript; CAC: designed the randomized controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation, interpreted the data, and reviewed and revised the manuscript; RS: designed the randomized controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation and critically reviewed the manuscript; EAM, AE, and JC: designed the randomized controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation and critically reviewed and revised the manuscript; TM and JR: conducted the research; RH: analyzed the breast-milk samples; CCG: conceived and designed the randomized controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation, analyzed and interpreted the data, and critically reviewed and revised the manuscript; and all authors: read and approved the final manuscript. title: The relationship between maternal self-efficacy, compliance and outcome in a trial of vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy
abstract: In a randomised controlled trial of vitamin D during pregnancy, we demonstrated that women with lower self-efficacy were more likely to experience practical problems with taking the trial medication, and that this was associated with lower compliance and achieved 25(OH)-vitamin D concentrations. Purpose-The relationship between self-efficacy (the belief that one can carry out a behaviour), compliance with study protocol and outcome was explored within a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy. Methods-In the MAVIDOS trial, women with circulating plasma 25(OH)-vitamin D of 25-100nmol/l in early pregnancy were randomised to either 1000IU cholecalciferol/day or matched placebo from 14 weeks until delivery. Circulating 25(OH)-vitamin D concentrations were assessed at 14 and 34 weeks' gestation. A sequential sub-sample completed Schwarzer's General

main_text: Introduction There is growing evidence that early growth and factors operating in utero or early infancy influence peak bone mass in adulthood, a known risk factor for osteoporosis. [1] A large, recent, population-based trial of vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy (MAVIDOS) has confirmed the efficacy of 1000IU/day cholecalciferol for maternal 25(OH)D repletion. Furthermore, although negative for the primary outcome of infant whole body bone mineral content by DXA, the trial demonstrated in a pre-specified secondary analysis, a greater than 0.5 SD increase in infant BMC in neonates born to supplemented compared to placebo mothers, and who delivered during the winter months. [2] The implication of these findings is that supplementing pregnant women with vitamin D may be an important population-wide public health intervention to help prevent osteoporosis. In order for vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy to have long-term impact on the prevalence of this disease, women have to be prepared to take supplements in sufficient quantity and with sufficient consistency that they achieve circulating 25(OH)D repletion throughout gestation. This paper describes an investigation of the individual factors that may present barriers to compliance with this requirement. Individual psychological characteristics, such as a sense of self-efficacy (the belief that one is capable of carrying out a specific behaviour), are important determinants of behaviour and ultimately of health status. [3] Having a higher sense of self-efficacy and hence a greater sense of control over life has been shown repeatedly to predict better physical health and greater longevity. [4] Self-efficacy has also been shown to mediate the effect of dietary interventions on nutrient status and dietary quality. [5] Higher self-efficacy enabled participants to respond to a nutrition intervention by increasing their fruit and vegetable consumption, because they believed they were capable of doing so. [6] In the context of the vitamin D supplementation trial described above, a participant's self-efficacy is likely therefore to describe her belief that she is able to take the action required by the study protocol, which specifies that she should take the study medication every day. [7] The difficulties of achieving compliance with nutrient supplementation recommendations in pregnancy are well documented. [8, 9] Factors known to affect compliance with such recommendations depend on the population under study, and include age, level of education and socio-economic status. [10] [11] [12] The most common reasons given for not taking supplements during pregnancy are simply forgetting to take them or being put off taking them by the side effects. [13, 14] Compliance with supplementation regimes appears to be better in participants in clinical trials, though there may be features of the way clinical trials are run and how they are staffed that affect both compliance with the intervention protocol and trial outcomes. [15] Very little is understood, however, about the role played by trial participants' psychological characteristics, and how they may affect compliance with trial protocols and hence change trial outcomes. This paper examines the relationship between women's self-efficacy and their circulating vitamin D concentration in late pregnancy in the context of a supplementation trial. It explores factors that may affect compliance with the study protocol and how this might affect the trial outcome. Analysis reported in this paper tests the hypothesis that having higher self-efficacy is associated with better compliance and with factors affecting compliance, which in turn increase women's vitamin D status at the end of the trial.Methods A detailed description of The Maternal Vitamin D Osteoporosis Study (MAVIDOS) has been previously published [16] . Briefly, MAVIDOS is a multicentre, double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial of vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy in the United Kingdom. The study was conducted in accordance with guidelines laid down in the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the Southampton and South West Hampshire Research Ethics Committee. MAVIDOS was registered prospectively (ISRCTN:82927713; EUDRACT:2007-001716-23); full approval from UK MHRA was granted, and written, informed consent obtained from all participants.Participants The MAVIDOS trial was run at three study sites in the UK. Pregnant women were recruited when in attendance for early pregnancy ultrasound screening. The participants in the substudy reported in this paper were from the sample of 1207 recruited at one of these sites [University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust, Southampton, UK]. Women were recruited if they were aged over 18 years, were having a singleton pregnancy, were of a gestation less than 17 weeks based on last menstrual period and had 25(OH)D between 25 and 100nmol/l and serum calcium<2.75mmol/l. Women with known metabolic bone disease, renal stones, hyperparathyroidism or hypercalciuria, those taking medication known to interfere with fetal growth, with fetal anomalies on ultrasonography and those taking more than 400 IU/day vitamin D supplementation were excluded. Eligible women with were recruited and randomised at 14 weeks' gestation (or as soon as possible before 17 weeks' gestation if recruited later) to either cholecalciferol 1000IU/day or matched placebo taken until delivery of the baby. Recruitment to this sub-study began part-way through the trial. A further sub-sample of women who had taken part in the trial were written to asking if they were prepared to be interviewed about their experience of taking part in the trial . The number of interviews was determined by the point at which saturation of topics was reached.Measures Biochemical-Reference blood samples were collected from the mother at 14 and 34 weeks' gestation, and stored at -80°C after processing. Measurement of plasma 25(OH)-D (Liaison RIA automated platform, Diasorin, Minnesota, USA) was undertaken centrally (MRC Human Nutrition Research, Cambridge, UK) in a single batch at the end of the study. Questionnaires-Women self-completed Schwarzer's General Self-Efficacy Scale at 14 and 34 weeks of pregnancy. The General Self-efficacy Scale used was a shortened, 5-item scale derived from the original 10-item scale, and measured the general sense that one can perform novel or difficult tasks, or cope with adversity in various domains of human functioning. [17] This shortened version has been used in previous studies of women of child-bearing age in the UK, has internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha statistic = 0.85), and has been shown to predict quality of diet. [18] Example items are 'I can always manage to solve difficult problems if I try hard enough' and 'I am calm when things are difficult because I know I can cope'. Women were asked to indicate on a 4-point scale how much they agreed with each item. Responses were coded from 1 (strongly disagree) to 4 (strongly agree) and summed to give an overall score. A higher score indicated higher levels of selfefficacy, with a range of possible scores from 5 to 20. At 34 weeks of pregnancy, women also completed a modified version of the Problematic Experiences of Therapy Scale (PETS). [19] This scale is a brief self-report measure that assesses the extent to which respondents perceive that they have been prevented from carrying out an intervention for common and plausible reasons. Three of the four sub-scales of this assessment were used and adapted to address issues women may have had taking the trial medication. Example scale items used in this study are 'I could not take my capsules because I was unsure how to do it properly' (uncertainty about medication sub-scale), 'I skipped taking my capsules because I was not sure if it was helping' (doubts about medication sub-scale), and 'lack of time prevented me from taking my capsules' (practical problems sub-scale). At the end of this questionnaire, women were then asked in an additional question how they had found taking the study medication and given space to write about their experiences. At 14 weeks of pregnancy, women were asked about their age, their level of education and amount of time spent outdoors, and their weights and heights were measured. [16] Compliance with the medication was assessed by pill count at the end of the trial and was defined as the percentage of pills taken of the number of pills each woman was expected to take. If this information was not available, proportions of pills taken at 19 or 34 weeks was used, whichever was the latest count. At the end of the trial, women who consented were interviewed at home by a member of the research team about their experiences of taking part in the trial and the interview transcripts subjected to a thematic analysis.Statistical analysis This sub-study was carried out in all women who provided data on self-efficacy at 14 weeks and had vitamin D data at 34 weeks. Distribution of circulating 25(OH)D at 34 weeks was checked to assess normality. Self-efficacy score and compliance were analysed as continuous variables. The three scores derived from the PETS, defined as "practical problems", "uncertainty about medication" and "doubts about medication" were dichotomised into having no problems or having at least one problem. The free text responses to the question about how they found taking the study medication were coded into one of six types of response: "all fine", "ok, built into daily routine", "occasionally forgot tablets", "issue with capsule size", "issue with illness", "forgot for short periods because of change in routine". Analyses were performed separately for intervention and placebo groups, and then for the two groups combined. The associations between predictors and 25(OH)D at 34 weeks were explored with linear regression. The results are displayed as β-coefficients with 95% confidence intervals (95% CI). Logistic regression was used to explore the association between self-efficacy and PETS variables describing problems with taking the medication, with estimates displayed as ORs (95% CIs). Mediation analyses were conducted to test the mediation effect of experiences of taking the study medication on the relationship between self-efficacy and 25(OH)D at 34 weeks. The mediation test was conducted according to the method proposed by Baron and Kenny. [20] Three regression models were fitted: first, the effect of self-efficacy on 25(OH)D at 34 weeks was tested; second, experiences of taking study medication (the mediators) were regressed on self-efficacy; and third, 25(OH)D at 34 weeks was regressed on both the mediators and self-efficacy. The mediation effect test is for an attenuation of the association between self-efficacy and 25(OH)D at 34 weeks after adjusting for experiences of taking the study medication. Analyses were carried out using Stata v 14.0 (Statacorp, College Station, Texas, USA).Results A total of 344 women completed the self-efficacy questionnaire at 14 weeks of pregnancy. Of those women, 328 provided 25(OH)-vitamin D measurements at 14 weeks and 218 at 34 weeks. Table 1 provides data on the age of this group of women, and their level of education. Mean self-efficacy at 14 weeks, and 25(OH)-vitamin D at 14 and 34 weeks of pregnancy are also shown, as are scores on the PETS scale and their percent compliance. The data are presented for control and treatment groups separately and for the whole group. The table shows that, in this sub-sample there were no differences between groups in these variables except in 25(OH)D at 34 weeks. Higher 25(OH)-vitamin D levels in the treatment group demonstrate the effectiveness of the intervention and suggest that this group of women was compliant with the trial protocol. Compliance with the medication regime was generally high, averaging 96% of pills taken during pregnancy. The table also shows, however, that of the 203 women who completed the PETS at 34 weeks, over half reported practical problems with taking the trial medication whereas fewer reported doubts or uncertainties about the medication. Table 2 shows that women entering the trial with higher self-efficacy were less likely (OR<1) to experience practical problems with taking the study medication, though they did not seem to be more likely to have doubts or uncertainties about whether it was right for them to be taking it. Neither was their lower self-efficacy associated with poorer compliance with the trial protocol. Experiencing practical problems, having doubts and uncertainties about the medication and rate of compliance were all strongly associated with one another (p < 0.05 for all). Women who experienced no doubts or uncertainties about taking the medication tended also to have no practical problems, and those who experienced no doubts, uncertainties or practical problems had higher rates of compliance (Table 3 ). For women in the treatment group, Table 4 shows that the more compliant they were, the higher their 25(OH)-vitamin D levels at 34 weeks. Figure 1 summarises the relationships between self-efficacy, experiences taking the study medication and 25(OH)-vitamin D status at 34 weeks. There were no mediation effects of experiences of taking study medication on the relationship between self-efficacy at 14 weeks of pregnancy and vitamin D levels at 34 weeks. In the combined group of women in both treatment and placebo groups, those who had higher self-efficacy on entering the study tended to have higher 25(OH)D status at 14 weeks of pregnancy (β = 1.04, 95%CI 0.05,2.03, p=0.04). As Table 4 shows, this was not the case by late pregnancy (34 weeks) in either the treatment or placebo groups. Circulating 25(OH)D levels increased less between 14 and 34 weeks in women with higher self-efficacy irrespective of their allocation, possibly because women with higher self-efficacy entered pregnancy with higher vitamin D levels (β = -1.73 nmol/l, 95%CI -3.40, -0.07, p = 0.04). In response to the additional question about how they had found taking the study medication, 78% of the women reported no issues or said that they had developed a daily routine that helped them to remember to take the medication. A small percentage (11%) said they occasionally forgot to take a tablet, and the remainder said that the size of the tablet was a problem, or that illness or a change in routine, such as a holiday, had prevented them from taking the tablets for a period. Qualitative interviews with 14 women who had taken part in the trial confirmed that there were no major difficulties in complying with the requirement to take the study medication every day. If the women did mention problems, they tended to be practical problems associated with taking the medication, primarily the large size of the capsules and remembering to take them every day, rather than experiencing doubts or uncertainties about the study or the medication. In response to a question about issues she might have had taking part in the study, one woman said: "The massive pills maybe. They were pretty hefty to try and get down." (ID 3744, lines 244-245) Another said that she had no difficulty swallowing the tablets, but sometimes forgot them: "The tablets were easy to take. It was actually … trying to remember that I had to take them."(ID 4081, lines 135-136) Both these issues may have affected their compliance with the study protocol. Some of the women who described experiencing these practical problems with the study medication found that developing a daily routine helped: "I was already taking Pregnacare, so I just took everything together." (ID 3718, line 71)Discussion This study was carried out in a sub-group of 203 women taking part in a large, multi-centre randomised, placebo-controlled trial of the effects of vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy on neonatal bone mineral content. Women who entered the study with a lower sense of self-efficacy tended to have lower levels of circulating 25(OH)D in early pregnancy, though not in late pregnancy in either treatment or control groups. The vitamin D levels of these women were more likely to increase over the course of pregnancy than those of women with higher self-efficacy, however, presumably because they had lower levels at baseline. Responses to the adapted PETS indicated that more than half the women experienced problems taking the study medication, but not because they doubted its usefulness or were uncertain how to take it. Problems taking the study medication tended to be about the size of the capsules or difficulty remembering to take them, but qualitative data suggested that women usually found ways of overcoming the practical problems they experienced. The data partially support the study hypothesis that having higher self-efficacy is associated with better compliance and with factors affecting compliance, which in turn improves vitamin D status. Though there was no direct effect of women's self-efficacy on their compliance, women with lower self-efficacy were more likely to experience practical problems taking the study medication. Experiencing practical problems was associated with poorer compliance with the study protocol which required women to take the medication every day; and poorer compliance was associated with lower levels of vitamin D in late pregnancy in the treatment group. Qualitative data indicated that the major factors affecting compliance were difficulties in remembering to take the study medication every day and in swallowing the tablets which came in large capsules.Strengths and limitations The women who took part in this study were a highly educated group as were the original MAVIDOS study participants with approximately three quarters in both the sub study and original study having A level qualifications or above. [2] As a highly educated group, participants might be expected to have both higher self-efficacy and 25(OH)D status than the general population and therefore not represent the general population of women of childbearing age in the UK. [21] Women in this sub-study had, in fact, a comparable mean selfefficacy score to women in previous studies who had a wider range of educational attainment. [22] The high levels of compliance with the study protocol, however, suggest that women in this study were highly motivated, and that their responses to taking the supplement may not represent those of the general population to routine maternal vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy.Implications of the study Women largely complied with the protocol of the trial described in this paper. Those with lower self-efficacy experienced more problems, and those who experienced more problems took less of the study medication. This finding suggests that improving the self-efficacy of participants in clinical trials may indirectly improve compliance. Ensuring compliance is a major issue in the success of clinical trials; substantial resources are invested in monitoring how well participants adhere to study protocols, because compliance has been shown to have significant effects on trial outcomes. [23] It is possible to improve self-efficacy through intervention. [24, 25] Self-efficacy, or the belief that one is capable of carrying out a specific behaviour, is acquired through a combination of watching others or learning about other's experience of achieving these behavioural goals, being told that these goals are possible and achievable, past experience of being able to behave in this way, and current physiological or mood state. [3] Increasing self-efficacy in order to improve compliance with trial protocols could therefore be achieved through an exploratory conversation between participants and members of staff recruiting them. Applying these findings to routine vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy, conversations to increase self-efficacy for taking pregnancy supplements would need to explore the practical problems that the participant might have in taking and remembering to take the medication, and to support them to identify ways of overcoming them. Some practical problems might relate to the physiological and mood changes associated with being pregnant, and examples of ways in which others have overcome these difficulties might increase participants' self-efficacy beliefs about their ability to adhere to the protocol. The reasons women gave for not taking the medication in this study are similar to those given by women to explain why they do not take folic acid or iron supplements during pregnancy. Women say they forget or that the side effects put them off. [13, 14] Though a proportion of the women in this study did say they forgot to take the tablets, few were worried about medication side-effects or had doubts about the value of the research in which they were taking part. This is important to know and encouraging, in the context of making recommendations for routine maternal vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy as a public health intervention for the purpose of improving maternal and infant health outcomes.Conclusions Women's level of self-efficacy is indirectly associated with their compliance with a trial protocol requiring them to take daily vitamin D supplements. Women who had higher selfefficacy had higher 25(OH)D levels when they came into the trial and experienced fewer problems taking the study medication. The more compliant the women in the treatment group were with the study protocol, the higher their vitamin D levels in late pregnancy. These findings have implications for understanding differences between participants, particularly in their level of self-efficacy, and the effect of these differences on trial outcomes, and for anticipating responses to recommendations for routine maternal vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy.Figure 1 . 1 Figure 1. title: 57. PERINATAL DNA METHYLATION AT THE RXRA PROMOTER IS ASSOCIATED WITH GESTATIONAL VITAMIN D SUPPLEMENTATION: RESULTS FROM THE MAVIDOS TRIAL
abstract: descriptive data on rates of total hip (THR) and total knee replacement (TKR) within a large national cohort of incident RA patients and describe the potential influence of age, sex, geographic region and socio-economic status. Methods: Patients with a first diagnosis of RA between 1995 and 2013 were identified from the Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD). Those with diagnoses of multiple types of inflammatory arthritis were excluded. First occurrence of subsequent primary THR and TKR were identified using validated (to hospital records) Read code lists and patients were followed up until date of first event (THR and TKR analysed separately), death, transference out of CPRD or end of study period. Unadjusted incidence rates were calculated per 1,000 personyears (PYs) and stratified by sex, age category (<55 years, 55-69 years and !70 years) geographic region (11 regions) and quintiles of the index of multiple deprivation (IMD) score. We also calculated 5year cumulative incidence among those surviving 5-years. Results: We included 23,830 patients with newly diagnosed RA, of whom 16,779 (70.4%) were female. The average age was 61 (IQR 50-72) and regional variation ranged from 556 to 3,260. There was a total occurrence of 884 THRs and 1,201 TKRs, at an overall incidence rate per 1000 PYs (95% CI) of 6.26 (5.86, 6.69) and 8.62 (8.14, 9.12), respectively. Among men, the rates of THR and TKR were 5.10 (4.45, 5.85) and 8.57 (8.01, 9.16), respectively and among women these were 6. 72 (6.24, 7.25) and 8.74 (7.86, 9.71), respectively. Rates of THR and TKR rose in a monotonic fashion according to age, from 2. 96 (2.52,  3.46) and 4.82 (4.26, 5.45), respectively for patients aged <55 years to 9. 53 (8.55, 10.62) and 11.32 (10.24, 12.52), respectively for patients aged !70 years. Rates of THR and TKR decreased in an approximately monotonic fashion according to socio-economic status, from 7. 51 (6.47, 8.73) and 9.09 (7.92, 10.42), respectively for those in the least deprived quintile of IMD to 5. 08 (4.03, 6.07) and 7.31 (6.02, 8.89), respectively for those in the most deprived quintile of IMD. Regional variation ranged from 3.82 (2.22, 6.58) to 7.60 (6.35, 9.10) for THR and from 6. 60 (5.59, 7.81) to 10.29 (8.75, 12.11)  for TKR. The 5-year cumulative incidence was 3.20% (2.89, 3.51) and 4.57% (4.20, 4.94) for THR and TKR, respectively. Conclusion: We provide generalizable estimates of incidence of THR and TKR in the English and Welsh RA patient population and note variation across several key demographic variables.
